narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your Year 1 grades placing you in the 6th decile at your school—solidly above the typical 5.7 average—and your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is a bit below the usual 3.21 for applicants. Your LSAT score of 32 also trails the average of about 37, while your upper-middle family income reflects a stable background uncommon among many peers. You've indicated plans to attend part-time, which only about 7% of students do compared to the overwhelming majority going full-time. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your chance of failing at 50%—a coin-flip risk that tipped the decision against admitting you, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on the first try. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 grades, which landed in the 3rd decile—well below the typical 5.5 average—strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of bar failure despite your earlier promise. Interestingly, your choice of part-time study pushed the prediction in a positive direction, as the model associates it with better odds of passing in some cases. Your stronger Year 1 performance also helped, nudging the outlook toward success and softening the overall assessment. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your Year 3 dip stands out as the biggest red flag, pulling you away from the profiles of consistent high performers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and exploring full-time options if feasible to align with the majority path. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor. You're clearly motivated, and with targeted improvements, law school doors can reopen for you.
